# Quickstart

Generate one C++ header, compile the sample program, run it.

## 1. Elaborate the packet constants header

From `spec_validate/`:

```bash
py -3 tools/codegen.py --target cpp --domain packet
```

This writes `include/ni_flit_constants.h` from `generated/ni_packet.json`.

## 2. Inspect the output

The header starts with a provenance banner:

```cpp
// ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
// AUTO-GENERATED by tools/codegen.py -- DO NOT EDIT
// Source:    spec_validate/generated/ni_packet.json
// Source SHA: ea56f053b2a8
// Generator version: v1.0.0
// Generated at: 2026-05-27T06:21:43Z
// ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
```

Followed by `namespace ni { ... }` with all packet constants.

## 3. Compile the sample

```bash
g++ -std=c++17 -I include examples/use_constants.cpp -o use_constants.exe
```

## 4. Run it

```bash
./use_constants.exe
```

Output (excerpt):

```
=== NI packet constants (from generated header) ===
FLIT_WIDTH       = 402
HEADER_WIDTH     = 50
PAYLOAD_WIDTH    = 352
FLIT_DATA_WIDTH  = 392
LINK_WIDTH       = 403

=== Header field bit positions ===
noc_qos   width=0 (reserved placeholder, not bit-addressable)
axi_ch    at [ 2: 0] (width 3)
dst_id    at [18:11] (width 8)
rob_idx   at [29:25] (width 5)
```

## 5. Detect drift

When a JSON SSoT changes, verify the committed artifacts are up to date:

```bash
py -3 tools/codegen.py --check
```

Exit code `0` means clean; `1` means at least one artifact needs to be re-elaborated and committed.
